verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “After these things were ended, Paul purposed in the Spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem, saying, After I have been there, I must also see Rome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So he sent into Macedonia two of them that ministered unto him, Timotheus and Erastus; but he himself stayed in Asia for a season.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the same time there arose no small stir about the Way.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He sends Timothy and Erastus into Macedonia, but himself remains at Ephesus.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having made a long enough stay in that city, he wishes to remove elsewhere again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how is it, that having from the first chosen to depart into Syria, he turns back to Macedonia?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He purposed,” it says, “in the Spirit,” showing that all (that he did) was done not of his own power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now he prophesies, saying, “I must also see Rome:” perhaps to comfort them with the consideration of his not remaining at a distance, but coming nearer to them again, and to arouse the minds of the disciples by the prophecy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At this point, I suppose, it was that he wrote his Epistle to the Corinthians from Ephesus, saying, “I would not have you ignorant of the trouble which came to us in Asia.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since he had promised to go to Corinth, he excuses himself on the score of having loitered, and mentions the trial relating the affair of Demetrius.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There arose no small stir about the Way.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see the renown (acquired)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They contradicted, it says: (then) came miracles, twofold: (then) again, danger: such is the way the threads alternate throughout the whole texture (of the history).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, which made silver temples of Diana, brought no small gain unto the craftsmen.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which made,” it says, “silver temples of Diana.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how is it possible that temples could be made of silver?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Perhaps as small boxes (κιβώρια.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the honor paid to this (Diana) in Ephesus; since, when (Hom. in Eph.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Prol.) their temple was burnt it so grieved them, that they forbade even the name of the incendiary ever to be mentioned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how, wherever there is idolatry, in every case we find money at the bottom of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both in the former instance it was for money, and in the case of this man, for money. (ch. xix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not for their religion, because they thought that in danger; no, it was for their lucrative craft, that it would have nothing to work upon.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the maliciousness of the man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was wealthy himself, and to him indeed it was no such great loss; but to them the loss was great, since they were poor, and subsisted on their daily earnings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nevertheless, these men say nothing, but only he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe: “Whom having collected, and the workmen of like occupation,” having themselves common cause with him, “he said, Sirs, ye know that by this craft we have our wealth” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25); then he brought the danger home to them, that we are in danger of falling from this our craft into starvation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Moreover ye see and hear, that not alone at Ephesus, but almost throughout all Asia, this Paul hath persuaded and turned away much people, saying that they be no gods, which are made with hands: so that not only this our craft is in danger to be set at naught; but also, that the temple of the great goddess Diana should be despised, and her magnificence should be destroyed, whom all Asia and the world worshippeth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they heard these sayings, they were full of wrath, and cried out, saying, Great is Diana of the Ephesians.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet the very things he spoke were enough to bring them to true religion: but being poor senseless creatures, this is the part they act.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if this (Paul being) man is strong enough to turn away all, and the worship of the gods is in jeopardy, one ought to reflect, how great must this man’s God be, and that he will much more give you those things, for which ye are afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Already (at the outset) he has secured a hold upon their minds by saying, “This Paul hath turned away much people, saying, that they be no gods, which are made with men’s hands.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See what it is that the heathen are so indignant at; because he said that “they which be made of men are no gods.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Throughout, he drives his speech at their craft.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then that which most grieved them he brings in afterwards.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, with the other gods, he would say, we have no concern, but that “the temple also of the great goddess Diana is in danger to be destroyed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, lest he should seem to say this for the sake of lucre, see what he adds: “Whom the whole world worshippeth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he showed Paul’s power to be the greater, proving all (their gods) to be wretched and miserable creatures, since a mere man, who was driven about, a mere tentmaker, had so much power.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the testimonies borne to the Apostles by their enemies, that they overthrew their worship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There (at Lystra) they brought “garlands and oxen.” (ch. xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he says, “This our craft is in danger to be set at naught.—Ye have filled (all) everywhere with your doctrine.” (ch. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So said the Jews also with regard to Christ: “Ye see how the world is going after Him” (John xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19); and, “The Romans shall come and take away our city.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And again on another occasion, “These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also.” (ch. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6).—“And when they heard these sayings, they were full of wrath.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Upon what was that wrath called forth?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On hearing about Diana, and about their source of gain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And cried out, saying, Great is Diana of the Ephesians.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the whole city was filled with confusion: and rushed with one accord into the theatre.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is the way with vulgar minds, any trivial occasion shall hurry them away and inflame their passions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it behooves to do (things) with (strict) examination.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But see how contemptible they were, to be so exposed to all (excitements)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having caught Gaius and Aristarchus, men of Macedonia, Paul’s companions in travel, they dragged them:” (here) again recklessly, just as did the Jews in the case of Jason; and everywhere they set upon them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Paul would have entered in unto the people, the disciples suffered him not,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 30) so far were they from all display and love of glory.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And certain of the Asiarchs, which were his friends, sent unto him, desiring him that he would not adventure himself into the theatre” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 31) to a disorderly populace and tumult.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Paul complies, for he was not vainglorious, nor ambitious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Some therefore cried one thing, and some another: for the assembly was confused.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is the nature of the multitude: it recklessly follows, like fire when it has fallen upon fuel; and the more part knew not wherefore they were come together.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they drew Alexander out of the multitude, the Jews putting him forward.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was the Jews that thrust him forward; but as providence ordered it, this man did not speak.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Alexander beckoned with the hand, and would have made his defence unto the people.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But when they knew that he was a Jew, all with one voice about the space of two hours cried out, Great is Diana of the Ephesians.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A childish understanding indeed! as if they were afraid, lest their worship should be extinguished, they shouted without intermission.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For two years had Paul abode there, and see how many heathen there were still!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when the town clerk had appeased the people, he said, Ye men of Ephesus, what man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians is temple-keeper of the great goddess Diana, and of the image which fell down from Jupiter?” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As if the thing were not palpable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With this saying first he extinguished their wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And of the Diopetes.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There was another sacred object (ἱερὸν) that was so called.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Either he means the piece of burnt earth or her image.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (is) a lie.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Seeing then that these things cannot be spoken against, ye ought to be quiet, and to do nothing rashly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ye have brought hither these men, which are neither robbers of churches, nor yet blasphemers of your goddess.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All this however he says to the people; but in order that those (workmen) also might become more reasonable, he says: “Wherefore if Demetrius, and the craftsman which are with him, have a matter against any man, the law is open, and there are deputies: let them implead one another.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if ye enquire anything concerning other matters, it shall be determined in a lawful assembly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we are in danger to be called in question for this day’s uproar, there being no cause, for which (matter) we shall not be able to give an account for this concourse.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A lawful assembly,” he says, for there were three assemblies according to law in each month; but this one was contrary to law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he terrified them also by saying, “We are in danger to be called to account” for sedition.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look again at the things said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “After these things were ended,” it says, “Paul purposed in the Spirit, when he had passed through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem,” saying, “After I have been there, I must also see Rome.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He no longer speaks here after the manner of a man, or, He purposed to pass through those regions, without tarrying longer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore does he send away Timothy and Erastus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of this I suppose he says, “Wherefore when we could no longer forbear, we thought it good to be left at Athens alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He sent away,” it says, “two of those who ministered to him” (1 Thess. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 1), both to announce his coming, and to make them more eager.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he himself tarried awhile in Asia.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Most of all does he pass his time in Asia; and with reason: there, namely, was the tyranny, of the philosophers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Afterwards) also he came and discoursed to them again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the same time” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23), for indeed the superstition was excessive. (a) “Ye both see and hear,” so palpable was the result that was taking place—“that not alone at Ephesus, but almost throughout all Asia, this Paul by persuading hath turned away,” not by violence: this is the way to persuade a city.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, what touched them closely, “that they be no gods which are made with hands.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He overturns, says he, our craft: (e) “From this work we have our wealth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hath persuaded.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How did he persuade—he, a man of mean consideration?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How prevail over so great a force of habit? by doing what—by saying what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not for a Paul (to effect this), it is not for a man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even this is enough, that he said, “They are no gods.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now if the impiety (of the heathen religions) was so easy to detect, it ought to have been condemned long ago: if it was strong, it ought not to have been overthrown so quickly. (b) For, lest they should consider within themselves (how strange), that a human being should have such power as this, and if a human being has power to effect such things, why then one ought to be persuaded by that man, he adds: (f) “not only is this our craft in danger to be set at naught, but also,” as if forsooth alleging a greater consideration, “the temple of the great goddess Diana,” etc. (c) “whom all Asia and the world worshippeth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27.) (g) “And when they heard, they were filled with wrath, and shouted, Great is Diana of the Ephesians!” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For each city had its proper gods. (d) They thought to make their voice a barrier against the Divine Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Children indeed, these Greeks! (h) And their feeling was as if by their voice they could reinstate the worship of her, and undo what had taken place!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the whole city,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See a disorderly multitude!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when Paul,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul then wished to enter in that he might harangue them: for he took his persecutions as occasions for teaching: “but the disciples suffered him not.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark, how great forethought we always find them taking for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the very first they brought him out that they might not (in his person) receive a mortal blow; and yet they had heard him say, “I must also see Rome.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it was providential that he so predicts beforehand, that they might not be confounded at the event.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they would not that he should even suffer any evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And certain of the Asiarchs besought him that he would not enter into the theatre.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing his eagerness, they “besought him:” so much did all the believers love him.—“And they drew Alexander,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This Alexander, why did he wish to plead?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was he accused?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, but it was to find an opportunity, and overturn the whole matter, and inflame the anger of the people.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But when they knew that he was a Jew, all with one voice about the space of two hours cried out, Great is Diana of the Ephesians.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the inordinate rage?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well, and with rebuke does the town clerk say, “What man is there that knoweth not how that the city of the Ephesians—” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 35) (coming to the point) which they were frightened about.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is it this, says he, that ye do not worship her?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, “That knoweth not” Diana, but, “our city,” that it always worshipped her.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Seeing then that these things cannot be spoken against.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then do ye make a question about them, as if these things were not plain? (b) Then he quietly chides them, showing that they had come together without reason.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And to do nothing rashly,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Showing that they had acted rashly. (a) “For ye have brought hither,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They wanted to make religion the pretext for what concerned their own money-making: (c) and it was not right on account of private charges to hold a public assembly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he put them to a nonplus, and left them not a word to say for themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There being no cause,” he says, “for this concourse, for which” (matter) “we shall not be able to give the account.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how prudently, how cleverly, the unbelievers (act).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus he extinguished their wrath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as it is easily kindled, so also is it easily extinguished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had thus spoken,” it says, “he dismissed the assembly.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou how God permits trials, and by them stirs up and awakens the disciples, and makes them more energetic?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not sink down under trials: for He Himself will “also make the way of escape, that we may be able to bear them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing so makes friends, and rivets them so firmly, as affliction: nothing so fastens and compacts the souls of believers: nothing is so seasonable for us teachers in order that the things said by us may be heard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the hearer when he is in ease is listless and indolent, and seems to suffer annoyance from the speaker: but when he is in affliction and distress, he falls into a great longing for the hearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when distressed in his soul, he seeks on all sides to find comfort for his affliction: and the preaching brings no small comfort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then,” you will say, “of the Jews?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How was it that in consequence of their weakheartedness, they did not hear?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, they were Jews, those ever weak and miserable creatures: and besides, the affliction in their case was great, but we speak of affliction in moderation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For observe: they expected to be freed from the evils that encompassed them, and they fell into numberless greater evils: now this is no common distress to the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Afflictions cut us off from the sympathy we have for the present world, as appears in this, that we wish for death immediately, and cease to be loving of the body: which very thing is the greatest part of wisdom, to have no hankering, no ties to the present life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soul which is afflicted does not wish to be concerned about many things: repose and stillness are all it desired, content for its part to have done with the things present, even though there be nothing else to follow.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As the body when wearied and distressed does not wish to indulge in amours, or gormandizing, but only to repose and lie down in quiet; so the soul, harassed by numberless evils, is urgent to be at rest and quiet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soul which is at ease is (apt to be) fluttered, alarmed, unsettled: whereas in this there is no vacuity, no running to waste: and the one is more manly, the other more childish; the one has more gravity, in the other more levity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as some light substance, when it falls upon deep water, is tossed to and fro, just so is the soul when it falls into great rejoicing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover, that our greatest faults arise out of overmuch pleasure, any one may see.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Come, if you will, let us represent to ourselves two houses, the one where people are marrying, the other where they are mourning: let us enter in imagination into each: let us see which is better than the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, that of the mourner will be found full of seriousness (φιλοσοφίας); that of the marrying person, full of indecency.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For look, (here are) shameful words, unrestrained laughter, more unrestrained motions, both dress and gait full of indecency, words fraught with mere nonsense and foolery: in short, all is ridicule there, all ridiculous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I do not say the marriage is this; God forbid; but the accompaniments of the marriage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then nature is beside itself in excess of riot.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Instead of human beings, those present become brute creatures, some neighing like horses, others kicking like asses: such utter license, such dissolute unrestraint: nothing serious, nothing noble: (it is) the devil’s pomp, cymbals, and pipes, and songs teeming with fornication and adultery.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But not so in that house where there is mourning; all is well-ordered there: such silence, such repose, such composure; nothing disorderly, nothing extravagant: and if any one does speak, every word he utters is fraught with true philosophy: and then the wonderful circumstance is, that at such times not men only, but even servants and women speak like philosophers—for such is the nature of sorrow—and while they seem to be consoling the mourner, they in fact utter numberless truths full of sound philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Prayers are there to begin with, that the affliction may stop there, and go no further: many a one comforting the sufferer, and recitals without number of the many who have the like cause for mourning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For what is man?” (they ask) (and thereupon) a serious examination of our nature—“aye, then, what is man!” (and upon this) an impeachment of the life (present) and its worthlessness, a reminding (one another) of things to come, of the Judgment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (So from both these scenes) each returns home: from the wedding, grieved, because he himself is not in the enjoyment of the like good fortune; from the mourning, light-hearted, because he has not himself undergone the like affliction, and having all his inward fever quenched.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what will you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall we take for another contrast the prisons and the theatres?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one is a place of suffering, the other of pleasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us again examine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the former there is seriousness of mind; for where there is sadness, there must needs be seriousness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He who aforetime was rich, and inflated with his own importance, now will even suffer any common person to converse with him, fear and sorrow, like some mightier fire, having fallen on his soul, and softening down his harshness: then he becomes humble, then of a sad countenance, then he feels the changes of life, then he bears up manfully against everything.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in a theatre all is the reverse of this—laughter, ribaldry, devil’s pomp, dissoluteness, waste of time, useless spending of days, planning for extravagant lust, adultery made a study of, practical training to fornication, schooling in intemperance, encouragement to filthiness, matter for laughter, patterns for the practice of indecency.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so the prison: there you will find humbleness of mind, exhoration, incentive to seriousness, contempt of worldly things; (these) are all trodden under foot and spurned and, fear stands over (the man there), as a schoolmaster over a child, controlling him to all his duties.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if you will, let us examine in a different way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I should like you to meet a man on his return from a theatre, and another coming out of prison; and while you would see the soul of the one fluttered, perturbed, actually tied and bound, that of the other you would see enlarged, set free, buoyant as on wings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the one returns from the theatre, enfettered by the sight of the women there, bearing about chains harder than any iron, the scenes, the words, the gestures, that he saw there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the other on his return from the prison, released from all (bounds), will no longer think that he suffers any evil as comparing his own case with that of (those) others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (To think) that he is not in bonds will make him thankful ever after; he will despise human affairs, as seeing so many rich men there in calamity, men (once) having power to do many and great things, and now lying bound there: and if he should suffer anything unjustly, he will bear up against this also; for of this too there were many examples there: he will be led to reflect upon the Judgment to come and will shudder, seeing here (in the earthly prison) how it will be there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as it is with one here shut up in prison, so in that world also before the Judgment, before the Day that is to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Towards wife, children, and servants, he will be more gentle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so he that comes from the theatre: he will look upon his wife with more dislike, he will be peevish with his servants, bitter towards his children, and savage towards all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great are the evils theatres cause to cities, great indeed, and we do not even know that they are great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall we examine other scenes of laughter also, I mean the feasts, with their parasites, their flatterers, and abundance of luxury, and (compare with them) places where are the halt and blind?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As before, in the former is drunkenness, luxury, and dissoluteness, in the latter the reverse.—See also with regard to the body, when it is hot-blooded, when it is in good case, it undergoes the quickest change to sickness: not so, when it has been kept low.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let me make my meaning clearer to you:—let there be a body having plenty of blood, plenty of flesh, plump with good living: this body will be apt even from any chance food to engender a fever, if it be simply idle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let there be another, struggling rather with hunger and hardship: this is not easily overcome, not easily wrestled down by disease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Blood, though it may be healthy in us, does often by its very quantity engender disease: but if it be small in quantity, even though it be not healthy, it can be easily worked off.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So too in the case of the soul, that which leads an easy, luxurious life, has its impulses quickly swayed to sin: for such a soul is next neighbor to folly, to pleasure, to vainglory also, and envy, and plottings, and slanderings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold this great city of ours, what a size it is!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence arise the evils? is it not from those who are rich? is it not from those who are in enjoyment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who are they that “drag” men “before the tribunals?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who, that dissipate properties?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those who are wretched and outcasts, or those who are inflated with consequence, and in enjoyment?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not possible that any evil can happen from a soul that is afflicted.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Paul knew the gain of this: therefore he says, “Tribulation worketh patience, and patience experience, and experience hope, and hope maketh not ashamed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not sink in our afflictions, but in all things give thanks, that so we may get great gain, that we may be well-pleasing to God, who permits afflictions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A great good is affliction: and we learn this from our own children: for without affliction (a boy) would learn nothing useful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we, more than they, need affliction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if there, when the passions (as yet) are quiet, (chastisement) benefits them, how much more us, especially possessed as we are by so many!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, we behoove rather to have schoolmasters than they: since the faults of children cannot be great, but ours are exceeding great.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Our schoolmaster is affliction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then not draw it down willingly upon ourselves, but when it is come let us bear it bravely, being, as it is, always the cause of numberless good things; that so we may both obtain grace from God, and the good things which are laid up for them that love Him, in Christ Jesus our Lord, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Spirit, be glory, might, honor, now and evermore, world without end.
